With self-preference, Job declared that he did not need to be taught by them. Those who dispute are tempted to magnify themselves and lower their brethren more than is fitting. When dismayed or distressed with the fear of wrath, the force of temptation, or the weight of affliction, we should apply to the Physician of our souls, who never rejects anyone, never prescribes amiss, and never leaves any case uncured. We may speak to Him at all times. To broken hearts and wounded consciences, all creatures without Christ are physicians of no value. Job evidently speaks with a very angry spirit against his friends. They had advanced some truths which nearly concerned Job, but the heart unhumbled before God never meekly receives the reproofs of men.

Job resolved to cleave to the testimony his own conscience gave of his uprightness. He depended upon God for justification and salvation, the two great things we hope for through Christ. He little expected temporal salvation, but of his eternal salvation he was very confident; that God would not only be his Savior to make him happy, but his salvation, in whose sight and enjoyment he should be happy. He knew himself not to be a hypocrite, and concluded that he would not be rejected. We should be well pleased with God as a Friend, even when he seems against us as an enemy. We must believe that all will work for good to us, even when all seems to make against us. We must cleave to God, yes, though we cannot for the present find comfort in him. In a dying hour, we must derive from him living comforts; and this is to trust in him, though he slay us.

Job begs to have his sins discovered to him. A true penitent is willing to know the worst of himself; and we should all desire to know what our transgressions are, that we may confess them and guard against them for the future. Job complains sorrowfully of God’s severe dealings with him. Time does not wear out the guilt of sin. When God writes bitter things against us, his design is to make us bring forgotten sins to mind, and so to bring us to repent of them as to break us off from them. Let young persons beware of indulging in sin. Even in this world they may possess the sins of their youth so that they have months of sorrow for moments of pleasure. Their wisdom is to remember their Creator in their early days, that they may have assured hope and sweet peace of conscience as the solace of their declining years. Job also complains that his present mistakes are strictly noticed. Far from it, God does not deal with us according to our deserts. This was the language of Job’s melancholy views. If God marks our steps and narrowly examines our paths in judgment, both body and soul feel his righteous vengeance. This will be the awful case of unbelievers, yet there is salvation devised, provided, and made known in Christ.
